quote: rank among the most frequent modal forms in the corpus (easkay 1,324 tokens, rank 139; eaykap 1,082 tokens, rank 169) ‹corpus-confirmed›
issue: A corpus-confirmed claim with token counts is stated without any source citation. The tag implies the data come from a specific corpus, but no Ref is provided.
suggestion: Add a source reference (e.g., the corpus name or a citation key) after the figures, or change the tag to match the evidential basis actually available.

quote: [131-133] 'In the auxiliary function the e- is part of the lexical form rather than a live derivational operation' (no tag) vs [319] 'the auxiliary function is grammaticalized and the e- is no longer a live derivation in this context ‹speculative›'
issue: The same claim about the frozen status of e- in the auxiliary function is given in two places with different epistemic grades: unmarked (implying consensus or established fact) in section 2, and ‹speculative› in section 4. This internal inconsistency needs to be resolved by choosing one uniform grade or explaining the difference.
suggestion: Harmonize the epistemic grading: either remove the ‹speculative› tag in line 319 if the claim is well supported, or add a comparable tag to the earlier passage if it is considered speculative.

quote: the read sources do not derive the circumstantial-obstacle semantics compositionally from this decomposition
issue: 'the read sources' is a phrase typical of the author’s production notes rather than a formal reference to the literature. It sounds as if the writer is saying 'the sources I have read' rather than introducing published work.
suggestion: Replace 'the read sources' with 'the available literature' or 'the sources consulted' to better suit scholarly prose.
